sections.heading: Clinical Follow-up Schedule
sections.content:
Year 1: Every 1–3 months (head and neck exam including mirror/fiberoptic).
Year 2: Every 2–6 months.
Years 3–5: Every 4–8 months.
>5 years: Every 12 months.
Frequency depends on risk of relapse, second primaries, and treatment sequelae. All patients who currently smoke should be advised to quit; former smokers should remain abstinent.

sections.heading: Imaging Strategy
sections.content:
Baseline post-treatment imaging: For locoregionally advanced disease, CT/MRI is recommended within 3–4 months after surgery to establish a new baseline. FDG-PET/CT should be performed within 3–6 months after definitive RT or systemic therapy/RT (optimally at 3 months) to assess treatment response [Cheung 2016, Heineman 2017].
Early imaging (before 8 weeks for CT/MRI, before 12 weeks for PET/CT) is associated with significant false-positive rates and should be avoided unless signs of recurrence or progression are present [IMG-A 3 of 4].
If FDG-PET/CT is negative at 3 months, further routine imaging in asymptomatic patients has limited benefit; a tailored approach based on tumor type, stage, symptoms, and exam changes is appropriate.
Annual imaging (CT/MRI) may be indicated for areas inaccessible to clinical examination (e.g., deep-seated skull base, areas obscured by treatment change).
For patients with high smoking history, annual low-dose chest CT may be considered for lung cancer screening.
Ultrasound of the neck is a safe, inexpensive, and accurate tool for nodal surveillance [Paleri 2016].
If clinical concern for metastatic disease is confined to a specific anatomical area, directed CT or MRI may be used.
For patients receiving immunotherapy, imaging every 2–3 cycles in the recurrent/metastatic setting is recommended [SYST-A 1 of 7].

sections.heading: Laboratory Monitoring
sections.content:
TSH every 6–12 months if neck irradiation and/or partial thyroidectomy.
For skull base RT: annual AM cortisol, GH, free T4, prolactin, LH/FSH, ACTH, TSH, total and bioavailable testosterone (category 2B) [VanKoevering 2020].
Consider EBV DNA monitoring for EBER+ nasopharyngeal cancer (category 2B).
For patients receiving cisplatin: routine monitoring of creatinine, magnesium, and audiogram as clinically indicated.
Hepatitis B screening for all patients undergoing cancer therapy; chronic HBV carriers should receive antiviral prophylaxis during and for at least 12 months after anticancer therapy.

sections.heading: Supportive Follow-up
sections.content:
Speech/hearing and swallowing evaluation and rehabilitation as clinically indicated.
Nutritional evaluation and rehabilitation until nutritional status is stabilized.
Ongoing surveillance for depression (NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management).
Smoking cessation and alcohol counseling as clinically indicated.
Lymphedema evaluation and rehabilitation as indicated (see NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship, SLYMPH-A).
For patients receiving or who received checkpoint inhibitor therapies, monitor for ongoing adverse reactions (see NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities).
Integration of survivorship care and care plan within 1 year, complementary to ongoing head and neck oncology follow-up [Cohen 2016].
Additional health monitoring and immunizations under primary care physician.

timeline.title: Baseline (0–6 months)
timeline.body: Post-treatment imaging: CT/MRI within 3–4 months after surgery; FDG-PET/CT within 3–6 months after RT (optimally at 3 months). Avoid early imaging (<8 weeks CT/MRI, <12 weeks PET/CT) unless signs of recurrence. Initiate clinical exam schedule.

timeline.title: Year 1
timeline.body: Head and neck exam (mirror/fiberoptic) every 1–3 months. Imaging as indicated (e.g., annual CT/MRI for inaccessible areas; low-dose chest CT for high smoking history). TSH every 6–12 months if neck irradiation/partial thyroidectomy. Skull base RT: annual endocrine panel. Consider EBV DNA for EBER+ nasopharyngeal CA. Supportive care: speech/swallow evaluation, nutrition, depression screening, smoking cessation, lymphedema management. For immunotherapy patients: imaging every 2–3 cycles in recurrent/metastatic setting. Hepatitis B screening and antiviral prophylaxis as needed.

timeline.title: Year 2
timeline.body: Head and neck exam every 2–6 months. Continue annual imaging as indicated (CT/MRI, chest CT). TSH monitoring every 6–12 months. Ongoing supportive care and surveillance for treatment sequelae.

timeline.title: Years 3–5
timeline.body: Head and neck exam every 4–8 months. Annual imaging for inaccessible areas or high-risk patients. TSH monitoring every 6–12 months if indicated. Continue supportive care and surveillance for late effects (e.g., lymphedema, depression, swallowing).

timeline.title: >5 years
timeline.body: Annual head and neck exam. Annual imaging for deep-seated/skull base areas, or if high smoking history (low-dose chest CT). TSH monitoring every 6–12 months if neck irradiation. Continue supportive care and integrate survivorship care plan (within 1 year). Additional health monitoring and immunizations under primary care physician.

timeline.title: Ongoing (all periods)
timeline.body: Smoking cessation and alcohol counseling as indicated. Nutritional and swallowing rehabilitation until stable. Depression surveillance per NCCN Distress Management. Lymphedema evaluation and rehabilitation. For checkpoint inhibitor patients: monitor for immunotherapy-related toxicities per NCCN guidelines. Annual skull base endocrine panel if applicable (AM cortisol, GH, free T4, prolactin, LH/FSH, ACTH, TSH, testosterone). EBV DNA monitoring for EBER+ nasopharyngeal CA (category 2B). Cisplatin patients: routine Cr, Mg, audiogram as needed. Hepatitis B antiviral prophylaxis for chronic carriers during and ≥12 months after therapy.
